title: DNMT1 knockout delivers a strong blow to genome stability and ...Long‐Term Perceived Benefit of Pediatric Cochlear Implant ...Rapid and transient recruitment of DNMT1 to DNA double-strand ...The impact of unilateral, simultaneous, or sequential ...The Impact of Cumulative Cochlear Implant Wear Time on Spoken ...
snippet: The DNA methyltransferase DNMT1 is essential for cell viability in various mouse models, but gene targeting in human tumor cells results in a viable line. This dilemma has now been resolved using a new human DNMT1 knockout line, although new questions arise as to the full extent of DNMT1 function in maintaining genome integrity. Download PDF DNMT1 is viewed as the maintenance DNA methyltransferase, as it interacts with proliferating cell nuclear antigen (PCNA) and is targeted to replication foci during S phase1. Mice deficient for Dnmt1 lose ∼90% of their DNA methylation and die early in embryogenesis2. Mouse embryonic stem cells deficient in Dnmt1, although viable, die when induced to differentiate, and Dnmt1 conditional knockout mouse fibroblasts die within a few cell divisions after gene deletion3,4. It was assumed that DNMT1 in human cells would also prove essential; however, HCT116 cells with disrupted DNMT1 continue to proliferate and maintain ∼80% of their genomic methylation5. This has fueled a controversy: was there a fundamental difference between humans and mice, or were there problems associated with the knockout strategy? Recent studies from three laboratories, including those of En Li and colleagues on page 391 of this issue6, have addressed this debate, and their results demonstrate that the requirement for DNMT1 in human tumor cells is remarkably similar to that of mouse cells. DNMT1 function revisited HCT116 cells deficient in DNMT1 have proven extremely useful and have been used in numerous studies to dissect the role of this molecule in mediating aberrant DNA hypermethylation in tumor cells5,7. Nevertheless, nagging questions remained: were they complete knockouts for DNMT1, and if so, were they rare clones adapted to loss of DNMT1 during selection? Recent work has shed light on this question, demonstrating that the DNMT1 HCT116 knockout cells were, in fact, hypomorphs8,9. The targeting strategy involved deleting exons 2–5 of DNMT1 and was predicted to throw the remainder of the transcript out of frame; however, alternate splicing produces a nearly full-length protein lacking a portion of its N-terminal domain that interacts with PCNA and DMAP1. Both studies also demonstrate that the truncated DNMT1, although expressed at reduced levels, is catalytically active, thus explaining why DNMT1-deficient HCT116 cells retain ∼80% of their genomic methylation. Notably, this truncated form of DNMT1 no longer interacts with PCNA or localizes with replication foci during S phase9, indicating that maintenance methylation can occur, albeit with reduced efficiency, without the presence of DNMT1 at replication foci. En Li and colleagues now enter the fray with a newly created HCT116 line engineered with an inducible knockout system6. Within 48 h after Cre-mediated deletion of DNMT1, the authors observed a complete loss of DNMT1 protein, a 90% reduction in DNMT activity and activation of the G2/M checkpoint. However, this arrest proved transient, and surviving cells exited mitosis in a tetraploid state with pronounced chromosomal defects and eventually underwent apoptosis. This DNMT1 requirement for cell viability agrees with results obtained when Dnmt1 is disrupted in mouse fibroblasts3 and when RNA interference is used to deplete the remaining DNMT1 from HCT116 hypomorphs8,9. Most prominently, these results indicate that the requirement for DNMT1 to maintain cell viability is conserved between humans and mice, and between tumor and normal cells.
